EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive trough provided strong forcing aloft to support a sub-980mb cyclone that trekked across the Great Lakes 1/9 into 1/10. This system dropped heavy, wet snow with snow-to-liquid ratios near 10:1 -- and even lower during the evening hours of 1/9. While most of northern Michigan saw at least several inches of snow, parts of northern lower saw between 8-12 by the morning of 1/10. Gusty winds of 25-45 mph also lead to blowing and drifting snow across northern Michigan.

Very strong troughing worked across the Mississippi Valley and over the southern Great Lakes 1/12 into 1/13, providing impressive forcing aloft to rapidly deepen a cyclone near 980mb as it worked across the southern half of Michigan and over Lake Huron by the morning of 1/13. This system produced a swath of 7 to 12 inches of snowfall across most of northern lower Michigan away from the Lake Michigan shoreline, with localized totals of 15 inches in West Branch and Hale. Wind gusts exceeding 40 mph across most of the area lead to blowing and drifting snow that resulted in significant travel disruptions.

As a strong storm system worked away from the Great Lakes, west-northwest winds spread across the region and generated lake effect snow across most of northwest lower and eastern upper Michigan. Cold air over the unusually warm Great Lakes waters supported robust lake effect snow bands across parts of the area, dropping over a foot of snow and resulting in rapid drops in visibility to 1/4 mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy lake effect snow affected far southwest Michigan during the afternoon hours of January 19th through the morning hours of January 20th. Snowfall totals of 8 to 18 inches were common, mainly over Berrien County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow with near zero visibility and intense snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour developed during the afternoon hours of January 19th. This dominant snow band did begin to weaken early January 20th.  Snowfall totals ranged between 12 and 18 inches in far southwest Berrien County to 8 to 15 inches in southeast Berrien County. Interstate 94 had traffic backed up for several hours due to numerous accidents and slide-offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal November temperatures in the mid 50s preceded a compact mid-level traversing southern lower MI over the latter half of the 10th. Cold cored system aided in deepening convective depths to generate a broken line of shallow thunderstorms and showers with several embedded circulations, one of which very briefly reached the ground on the south end of Holly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wall damage, foundation damage, and a bent door to residential property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cyclonic flow settled into the Upper Great Lakes starting on Thanksgiving night as the first in a series of disturbances moved into the region. Upper level troughing then settled in for the weekend, spanning the last couple days of November and the first couple days of December, bringing an unseasonably cold air mass over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, resulting in an extended period of significant lake effect snow. Despite wind shifts that shifted the position of the most intense snow bands through the weekend, 1-3inch per hour snowfall rates within these bands resulted in 12hour totals of 12inches or more. The NWS Gaylord, MI office, located 9miles SSW of Gaylord, set an all-time calendar-day snowfall record of 24.8inches for the calendar date of 11-29-2024, with more to come through the next 3 days into December 2. Some areas of Otsego County received as much as 4feet of snow through the entire event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer from 1mile ESE of Mancelona reported a 72-hour snowfall total of 31.4 inches of snow ending December 1. Most intense snowfall occurred on the 29th, with a 24-hour total of 14.2 inches ending at 0800EST on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightening pressure gradient in the wake of a Clipper low pressure system moving through the Upper Great Lakes led to damaging west to north wind gusts over portions of  Upper Michigan on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed power line was reported on Washington Avenue in Stambaugh.  The Iron River Police Department had to reroute traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low pressure system moved into the Upper Great Lakes late in the day on November 25, 2024, bringing accumulating wet snow to the eastern U.P. of Michigan. Accumulations were minimized south of the Mackinac Bridge due to warmer temperatures and precipitation falling as rain or a mix at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of eight to ten inches of snow in the Kincheloe/Kinross, MI area overnight, from social media and passed along by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted the Great Lakes region on Friday Jan 12 2024 into Saturday Jan 13 2024. Precipitation quickly overspread southeast Michigan during the mid afternoon hours of Friday as the main low (981 MB)  reached Chicago in the early evening hours. Just enough mild air near Lake Erie/Detroit River caused some melting snow/rain. Otherwise, despite temperatures mainly 32-34 degrees, heavy wet snow was the predominant precipitation type during the late afternoon into the early evening hours. This was due to very strong forcing, which led to visibility down around a quarter of a mile in the heavy snow, with even thundersnow reported in and around the Detroit Metro Area.  Storm totals reached 4-8 inches across the Detroit Metro Area, as well as the Tri-Cities region and Lapeer county, with 1-5 inches elsewhere. Precipitation did taper off in the mid to late evening, and fell in the form of rain south of I-69 where the total liquid equivalent ranged from 0.5-1.25���. Here are some of the highest snowfall totals received: |Ortonville           8.5 inches.|Lapeer (2W)       8.2 inches.|Midland (2E)       8.0 inches.|Ann Arbor           6.3 inches. |Auburn               5.8 inches. |Saginaw             5.6 inches. | |Due to the heavy wet nature of the snow, widespread tree limbs came down in several counties (Oakland/Lapeer to name a few), which caused power outages to well over 100,000 customers at the peak of the storm. Easterly winds also reached 30-40 mph ahead of the low, with southwest wind gusts topping out 40-55 mph south of M-59 Saturday morning as the low exited northeast.

An arctic airmass overspread southern Lower Michigan bringing dangerous wind chills. The combination of very cold temperatures in the single digits below zero and gusty west to southwest winds of 20 to 30 mph produced wind chills as cold as 25 below to 30 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of temperatures in the single digits below zero and gusty southwest winds of around 20 mph produced minimum wind chills to around 30 below zero during the morning hours. A wind chill of 31 below zero was reported by an AWOS station in Coldwater, MI at 9:03am January 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted the Great Lakes region on Friday Jan 12 2024 into Saturday Jan 13 2024. Precipitation quickly overspread southeast Michigan during the mid afternoon hours of Friday as the main low (981 MB)  reached Chicago in the early evening hours. Just enough mild air near Lake Erie/Detroit River caused some melting snow/rain. Otherwise, despite temperatures mainly 32-34 degrees, heavy wet snow was the predominant precipitation type during the late afternoon into the early evening hours. This was due to very strong forcing, which led to visibility down around a quarter of a mile in the heavy snow, with even thundersnow reported in and around the Detroit Metro Area.  Storm totals reached 4-8 inches across the Detroit Metro Area, as well as the Tri-Cities region and Lapeer county, with 1-5 inches elsewhere. Precipitation did taper off in the mid to late evening, and fell in the form of rain south of I-69 where the total liquid equivalent ranged from 0.5-1.25���. Here are some of the highest snowfall totals received: |Ortonville           8.5 inches.|Lapeer (2W)       8.2 inches.|Midland (2E)       8.0 inches.|Ann Arbor           6.3 inches. |Auburn               5.8 inches. |Saginaw             5.6 inches. | |Due to the heavy wet nature of the snow, widespread tree limbs came down in several counties (Oakland/Lapeer to name a few), which caused power outages to well over 100,000 customers at the peak of the storm. Easterly winds also reached 30-40 mph ahead of the low, with southwest wind gusts topping out 40-55 mph south of M-59 Saturday morning as the low exited northeast.

Northeast winds off Lake Huron and mesoscale circulation lead to narrow intense snow bands. Total snow accumulation reached 5-10 inches over parts of Huron and Sanilac counties. Here are a few of the higher totals received:|Deckerville              10 inches.|Sandusky                10 inches.|Harbor Beach (6S)     8 inches.

Very strong troughing worked across the Mississippi Valley and over the southern Great Lakes 1/12 into 1/13, providing impressive forcing aloft to rapidly deepen a cyclone near 980mb as it worked across the southern half of Michigan and over Lake Huron by the morning of 1/13. This system produced a swath of 7 to 12 inches of snowfall across most of northern lower Michigan away from the Lake Michigan shoreline, with localized totals of 15 inches in West Branch and Hale. Wind gusts exceeding 40 mph across most of the area lead to blowing and drifting snow that resulted in significant travel disruptions.

Broad troughing encompassed most of the eastern CONUS as an embedded shortwave worked over the Great Lakes 1/16 into 1/17. A cyclone progressing across Ontario kept west-southwest low-level winds in place for an extended period of time through 1/17. This helped focus lake effect snow across the far northern lower peninsula, dropping over a foot of snow across parts of Emmet and Cheboygan counties. The highest reported snowfall was 15 north of Indian River near Burt and Mullett Lakes. Gusty winds during the afternoon of 1/17 lead to whiteout conditions and dangerous travel at times.

An impressive trough provided strong forcing aloft to support a sub-980mb cyclone that trekked across the Great Lakes 1/9 into 1/10. This system dropped heavy, wet snow with snow-to-liquid ratios near 10:1 -- and even lower during the evening hours of 1/9. While most of northern Michigan saw at least several inches of snow, parts of northern lower saw between 8-12 by the morning of 1/10. Gusty winds of 25-45 mph also lead to blowing and drifting snow across northern Michigan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures climbing into the low to mid 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s Tuesday afternoon lead to heat indices reaching around 100 degrees across most of southeast Michigan. This hot and humid airmass fueled an expanding line of strong to severe thunderstorms which moved through southeast Michigan just before 4 PM and 7 PM. Wind gusts of 40-65 MPH impacted the majority of southeast Michigan, downing tree limbs and trees. Isolated stronger gusts between 66-76 mph were also observed across the Detroit Metro Area. In total, about 380,000 customers lost power in Michigan, with around 210,000 in the DTE service area/southeast Michigan region. Just about every county in southeast Michigan experienced at least minor tree damage. Due to the rapid movement of the line of thunderstorms, rainfall totals were limited to under 1 inch across the majority of the area. Much heavier rainfall occurred with thunderstorms moving through Washtenaw and Wayne counties Tuesday night, leading to total rainfall totals around 3 inches in Ann Arbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm lifting up from the Southern Plains and tracking through Lower Michigan dumped heavy snow over portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 12th into the 14th.  As much as two to three feet of heavy snow fell over portions of Baraga and Marquette counties,  greatest from the Huron Mountains through the higher terrain of western Marquette County.  North to northeast winds gusting as high as 50 mph also caused blizzard conditions in snow and blowing snow over portions of Marquette County on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant storm total lake effect snowfall amounts during the event included nearly three feet of snow at Mount Arvon, 19 inches at Herman and 11 inches just south of Baraga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm lifting up from the Southern Plains and tracking through Lower Michigan dumped heavy snow over portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 12th into the 14th.  As much as two to three feet of heavy snow fell over portions of Baraga and Marquette counties,  greatest from the Huron Mountains through the higher terrain of western Marquette County.  North to northeast winds gusting as high as 50 mph also caused blizzard conditions in snow and blowing snow over portions of Marquette County on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media snowfall total reports in less than 24 hours included 7.5 inches near Pine Stump Junction and 7.0 inches at Dollarville.  North winds gusting between 35 and 40 mph at times also resulted in blowing and drifting of snow and occasional whiteout conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures climbing into the low to mid 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s Tuesday afternoon lead to heat indices reaching around 100 degrees across most of southeast Michigan. This hot and humid airmass fueled an expanding line of strong to severe thunderstorms which moved through southeast Michigan just before 4 PM and 7 PM. Wind gusts of 40-65 MPH impacted the majority of southeast Michigan, downing tree limbs and trees. Isolated stronger gusts between 66-76 mph were also observed across the Detroit Metro Area. In total, about 380,000 customers lost power in Michigan, with around 210,000 in the DTE service area/southeast Michigan region. Just about every county in southeast Michigan experienced at least minor tree damage. Due to the rapid movement of the line of thunderstorms, rainfall totals were limited to under 1 inch across the majority of the area. Much heavier rainfall occurred with thunderstorms moving through Washtenaw and Wayne counties Tuesday night, leading to total rainfall totals around 3 inches in Ann Arbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm lifting up from the Southern Plains and tracking through Lower Michigan dumped heavy snow over portions of west and central Upper Michigan from the 12th into the 14th.  As much as two to three feet of heavy snow fell over portions of Baraga and Marquette counties,  greatest from the Huron Mountains through the higher terrain of western Marquette County.  North to northeast winds gusting as high as 50 mph also caused blizzard conditions in snow and blowing snow over portions of Marquette County on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wide ranging storm total snow amounts were reported across the county during the blizzard event.  Snow amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches near Lake Superior to as much as two to nearly three feet of snow over the higher terrain of western Marquette County. The Ishpeming and National Mine areas received the most snow with anywhere from 27 to 33 inches of storm total snow while the NWS Marquette office measured 24.4 inches.  North to northeast winds gusting over 50 mph at times also caused blizzard conditions in snow and blowing snow throughout much of the 13th.  The heavy snow also resulted in minor accidents and vehicles getting stranded on the 13th.

Just about every county in southeast Michigan experienced at least minor tree damage. Due to the rapid movement of the line of thunderstorms, rainfall totals were limited to under 1 inch across the majority of the area. Much heavier rainfall occurred with thunderstorms moving through Washtenaw and Wayne counties Tuesday night, leading to total rainfall totals around 3 inches in Ann Arbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees blown down across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures climbing into the low to mid 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s Tuesday afternoon lead to heat indices reaching around 100 degrees across most of southeast Michigan. This hot and humid airmass fueled an expanding line of strong to severe thunderstorms which moved through southeast Michigan just before 4 PM and 7 PM. Wind gusts of 40-65 MPH impacted the majority of southeast Michigan, downing tree limbs and trees. Isolated stronger gusts between 66-76 mph were also observed across the Detroit Metro Area. In total, about 380,000 customers lost power in Michigan, with around 210,000 in the DTE service area/southeast Michigan region. Just about every county in southeast Michigan experienced at least minor tree damage. Due to the rapid movement of the line of thunderstorms, rainfall totals were limited to under 1 inch across the majority of the area. Much heavier rainfall occurred with thunderstorms moving through Washtenaw and Wayne counties Tuesday night, leading to total rainfall totals around 3 inches in Ann Arbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines reported down across many parts of Washtenaw County, but especially over Scio Township and the Ann Arbor area. A few trees landed on houses and cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitter cold Arctic air funneling into the area on the backside of a low stationed over southern Hudson Bay resulted in dangerously cold wind chills across porttions of Upper Michigan from late evening on the 15th into the morning of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitter cold wind chills between 25 to 34 below zero were reported across portions of Gogebic County.  Schools were either closed or delayed on the 16th to the effects of the cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitter cold Arctic air funneling into the area on the backside of a low stationed over southern Hudson Bay resulted in dangerously cold wind chills across porttions of Upper Michigan from late evening on the 15th into the morning of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitter cold wind chills between 25 to 30 below zero were reported from Marquette to K.I Sawyer and west to Ishpeming and Negaunee.  Bitter cold wind chills between 25 to 34 below zero were reported across portions of Gogebic County.  Many schools across the county were closed on the 16th due to the effects of the cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cyclonic flow settled into the Upper Great Lakes starting on Thanksgiving night as the first in a series of disturbances moved into the region. Upper level troughing then settled in for the weekend, spanning the last couple days of November and the first couple days of December, bringing an unseasonably cold air mass over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, resulting in an extended period of significant lake effect snow. Despite wind shifts that shifted the position of the most intense snow bands through the weekend, 1-3inch per hour snowfall rates within these bands resulted in 12hour totals of 12inches or more. The NWS Gaylord, MI office, located 9miles SSW of Gaylord, set an all-time calendar-day snowfall record of 24.8inches for the calendar date of 11-29-2024, with more to come through the next 3 days into December 2. Some areas of Otsego County received as much as 4feet of snow through the entire event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four hour snowfall of 8 inches ending on the morning of the 30th, from Boyne Falls cooperative observer. Event totals as high as 16 to 17 inches in the Boyne Falls area by the morning of the 30th per social media reports. A social media report of a 32-inch snowfall total ending 0900EST November 30, northwest of Vanderbilt in Charlevoix county. A twenty-four hour snowfall total of 13.8inches measured at East Jordan Wastewater Treatment Plant ending on the morning of the 30th. As an example of lake effect snowfall gradients, the cooperative observer 2miles NW of East Jordan received only 10.2 inches through the entire 72+hour event ending the morning of December 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought significant snow followed by cold and windy conditions that resulted in persistent lake effect snow. Significant travel impacts occurred due to a long stretch of icy conditions that resulted from temperatures remaining well below freezing and light to moderate snow showers.  Up to two to two and a half feet of snow fell mainly near to west of Grand Rapids from the 12th through the 17th.  Numerous traffic accidents and school closings occurred across the area as a result.  Wind chill readings plummeted to as low as twenty to thirty degrees below zero late on January 14th and during the morning hours of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coop observer near Ludington reported six inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, convergent lake effect snow band pummelled the Keweenaw Peninsula with multiple days of moderate to heavy snow accumulation from the 13th into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total lake effect snowfall over a five-day period included 38.6 inches at Calumet-Tamarack, 36.2 inches at Painesdale and 32.8 inches at Quincy Hill.  North winds gusting over 35 mph at times caused considerable blowing and drifting of snow and lowered visibility. Snow drifts were reported to be more than four feet in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low pressure system moved into the Upper Great Lakes late in the day on November 25, 2024, bringing accumulating wet snow to the eastern U.P. of Michigan. Accumulations were minimized south of the Mackinac Bridge due to warmer temperatures and precipitation falling as rain or a mix at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos of private citizen's snow stake in Eckerman, MI area via social media. Initial update around 1920EST November 25 with approximately 5 inches of new snow. Updated again at 0455EST November 26 with approximately 8 inches total. Trout Lake received ten inches of snowfall overnight. Tahquamenon Falls State Park had a Twenty-four-hour snowfall of 8.5 inches ending November 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cyclonic flow settled into the Upper Great Lakes starting on Thanksgiving night as the first in a series of disturbances moved into the region. Upper level troughing then settled in for the weekend, spanning the last couple days of November and the first couple days of December, bringing an unseasonably cold air mass over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, resulting in an extended period of significant lake effect snow. Despite wind shifts that shifted the position of the most intense snow bands through the weekend, 1-3inch per hour snowfall rates within these bands resulted in 12hour totals of 12inches or more. The NWS Gaylord, MI office, located 9miles SSW of Gaylord, set an all-time calendar-day snowfall record of 24.8inches for the calendar date of 11-29-2024, with more to come through the next 3 days into December 2. Some areas of Otsego County received as much as 4feet of snow through the entire event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow began on the 28th. A period of intense snow associated with a vigorous lake effect snow band was noted between 0100EST and 1300EST on the 29th where 13inches fell in 12 hours, measured by the official snow observer on the SE side of town. Additional public reports indicate snowfall totals on the 29th could have been as high as 2-3feet in some areas where the band persisted longer closer to the Soo Locks. Another, though less, intense period was noted between 1300EST on the 30th through midnight on the 1st, where 7 inches fell in 12hrs, as this lake effect band wavered over time. Highest recorded snow depth for this period was 26 inches, achieved by midnight on December 1. 72-hour snowfall total of 26.2 inches ending 1900EST November 30th. A report from Sugar Island had accumulating snow beginning on the 28th, with accumulations around a foot by morning on the 29th...and enough to bring snow totals up to 20 inches by 4pm. By the afternoon of December 1st, 60-hour snowfall totals reached as high as 42 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought periods of snow and windy conditions during the daytime of January 12th. More snow and very windy conditions developed during the early morning hours of January 13th. Storm total snow accumulations of 6 to 11 inches were reported across much of southwest Lower Michigan, with 3 to 6 inches of storm total snow accumulation across south central Lower Michigan. The combination of snow and blowing snow produced dangerous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few rounds of snowfall occurred across the area. The first period of snow was during the daytime of January 12th. After a lull during the evening hours of January 12th, lake enhanced and lake effect snow moved back in during the early morning hours of January 13th. Storm total snowfall accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were reported.  A Cocorahs observer 2.4 miles northeast of Three Rivers, MI reported 6.9 inches of storm total snow accumulation. Strong west to southwest winds gusting as high as 40 to 45 mph on the morning of January 13th produced significant blowing and drifting snow resulting in slick road conditions and limited visibilities at times. Numerous accidents and slide-offs were reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the morning of August 6, a supercell developed and tracked east-southeast through southern Michigan, far northeast Indiana and far northwest Ohio. This storm produced an EF2  tornado in northwest St. Joseph county from Lewis Lake to the north side of Three Rivers. This storm did weaken with eastward extent but did maintain some rotation with reports of wall clouds and funnel clouds. Additional thunderstorms then developed east of I-69 Tuesday afternoon, with a few reports of wind damage in northwest Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down near Territorial road and Hillsdale Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high temperatures were set across the area on February 27th, and the unseasonably warm and relatively humid airmass set the stage for an early season severe weather event.  A line of severe thunderstorms moved in from the southwest |and several supercell thunderstorms developed as well. ||One long lived supercell with a persistent mesocyclone caused damage along the I-94 corridor which included an EF-1 Tornado near Marshall Michigan. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Michigan during the evening of February 27th into the early morning of February 28th, preceded by summer-like, record high temperatures on the 27th, and followed by seasonably cold, winter-like conditions on the 28th. Across northern Michigan, primarily small hail was reported, up to nickel-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance passing through the region enhanced WNW flow lake effect snow during the evening of February 16th. Snow to liquid ratios were approximately 20-to-1 with a glancing blow of arctic air. A more focused area of snow developed and persisted over northern Grand Traverse county during the evening into the early overnight hours.

Record high temperatures were set across the area on February 27th, and the unseasonably warm and relatively humid airmass set the stage for an early season severe weather event.  A line of severe thunderstorms moved in from the southwest |and several supercell thunderstorms developed as well. ||One long lived supercell with a persistent mesocyclone caused damage along the I-94 corridor which included an EF-1 Tornado near Marshall Michigan. Along with this supercell, a line of storms brought hail up to two and a half inches in diameter to a few locations south of I-96.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near sixteen and a quarter mile road and moved east northeast and uprooted or snapped hundreds of trees along its path. The tornado heavily damaged a few outbuildings and caused roof damage to a number of homes with shingles ripped off and tore away siding.  The tornado ended southwest of twenty one and a half mile road and L drive north. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record February high temperatures in the lower 70s created enough instability to allow for strong thunderstorms as a very strong cold front pushed through during the overnight hours. A few thunderstorms reached severe limits, highlighted by an EF-2 tornado in Grand Blanc. Winds behind the cold front on February 28th frequently gusted between 40-50 mph throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in Creasey Bicentennial Park damaging the south pavilion and uprooting a pine tree. The tornado tracked east northeast across Westminster Cir where multiple trees were snapped, two garage doors were blown out, and a roof was partially stripped from one home. The tornado continued tracking east northeast across Porter Rd and Reid Rd where multiple limbs and trees were snapped. The peak of damage occurred at an industrial complex at the intersection of Reid and S Dort Hwy. Damage to the complex included blown out non load bearing walls and loss of the majority of the roof. Peak winds were estimated at 115 mph.  Multiple transmission poles were also snapped along S Dort Hwy. Damaged continued into the Indian Hill neighborhood near downtown Grand Blanc where multiple large trees were downed with some of them falling onto homes along Old Bridge Rd to Stonybrook Dr. The tornado tracked east northeast across Genesee Road to Belsay Rd snapping hardwood limbs and uprooting pine trees before greater damage occurred along Moonstone Dr and Brookview Dr. Damage in these areas included more widespread tree damage and one home with a garage door blown out and a roof partially uplifted. The tornado weakened and lifted shortly after it crossed Perry Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal November temperatures in the mid 50s preceded a compact mid-level traversing southern lower MI over the latter half of the 10th. Cold cored system aided in deepening convective depths to generate a broken line of shallow thunderstorms and showers with several embedded circulations, one of which very briefly reached the ground on the south end of Holly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began in a wooded marshland southwest of Holly and continued northeast into the Village of Holly before dissipating just east of the center of town near the intersection of Martha St. and Washington St. Sporadic concentrated small tree and large limb damage, loss of roofing material on several facilities, construction site damage, and minor siding and fascia loss were observed along the portions of Tenny st, Legrande St, Sherman St, Saginaw St, Church St, Martha St, And Washington St. |Peak winds were estimated to be 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to two inches in diameter was reported in and near Oakwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening hours of May 7th, resulting in several reports of damaging wind gusts and large hail.  An EF-2 tornado struck southern Kalamazoo county as well resulting in twelve injuries and hundreds of millions of dollars in damage to area homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado touched down near the intersection of South 10th Street and West R Avenue and traveled east northeast for around 11 miles until it lifted north of East N Avenue just west of 31st street.  Many homes, including two mobile home parks, were severely damaged.  Over 500 homes were impacted, 305 of which were in Portage.  Twenty three of those homes were destroyed.  A number of businesses had significant damage.  Thousands of trees were either snapped or uprooted.  A large section of the roof of an industrial warehouse also collapsed near the Kalamazoo/Battle Creek international airport.  Twelve people were injured.  There were no fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cyclonic flow settled into the Upper Great Lakes starting on Thanksgiving night as the first in a series of disturbances moved into the region. Upper level troughing then settled in for the weekend, spanning the last couple days of November and the first couple days of December, bringing an unseasonably cold air mass over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, resulting in an extended period of significant lake effect snow. Despite wind shifts that shifted the position of the most intense snow bands through the weekend, 1-3inch per hour snowfall rates within these bands resulted in 12hour totals of 12inches or more. The NWS Gaylord, MI office, located 9miles SSW of Gaylord, set an all-time calendar-day snowfall record of 24.8inches for the calendar date of 11-29-2024, with more to come through the next 3 days into December 2. Some areas of Otsego County received as much as 4feet of snow through the entire event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall intensity began to increase at NWS Gaylord, MI overnight on the 28th-29th, reaching 9 inches by 0700EST. High snow-to-liquid ratios and intense, generally persistent bands with consistent 1 inch per hour snowfall rates resulted in the accumulation of 24.8 inches of snow by midnight on the 30th...setting a new calendar day record for the Gaylord area. Most intense snowfall occurred on the 29th into the morning of the 30th, with much less impressive snowfall rates for the remainder of the weekend in the immediate area of the office...with a 60-hr snowfall total of 33.8 inches ending 1900EST on the 30th. Highest snow depth during this period was 21 inches on the 30th...with an event total of 45.4inches by 1900EST December 2nd. Travel impacts were somewhat widespread, particularly focused beneath the snow bands, where whiteout conditions and significant snow accumulations on roadways made travel difficult to nearly impossible. A COOP observer at Pigeon River State Forest Headquarters reported a 24 hour total of 12.5 inches of snow ending the morning of the 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado with maximum wind speeds of 95 mph occurred in the early evening of May 7th in Cass county, MI. The tornado touched down in a field just north of the Dowagiac Municipal Airport. The tornado did damage to trees along Middle Crossing Rd, even uprooting a few large trees onto homes in the area. The tornado continued northeast, where a power pole was snapped and a few center pivots were flipped in a field adjacent to the Amtrak railroad near the intersection of Gage Street and Atwood Road.  The tornado reached its maximum width in the Twin Lakes area, where extensive damage to trees was noted on all sides of both lakes.  Hundreds of trees of varying sizes were snapped and uprooted in Twin Lakes. The tornado continued northeast, snapping trees on Dewey Lake Street. Additional damage to a barn was noted on Wickett Road. The tornado dissipated near the Cass/Van Buren county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe tornadoes, large hail, and damaging wind gusts on the afternoon and evening of May 7. The largest hail stone reported was 4 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-1 tornado developed southwest of Union City, MI as the Colon/Sherwood tornado to the northwest dissipated. This tornado moved along W Girard Road between Babcock and Arbogast Road, destroying a machine shed and ripping out of the anchor posts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe tornadoes, large hail, and damaging wind gusts on the afternoon and evening of May 7. The largest hail stone reported was 4 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in St. Joseph county on the south side of Centreville and then tracked northeast, passing north of Colon and into Branch county near Havens and Goodrich Lake. The tornado finally weakened and lifted south of Union Lake. Extensive damage occurred to trees and center pivot irrigation systems along the track with the most significant damage occurring from north of Colon to north of Sherwood, when video evidence indicated the tornado became multi-vortex. Several homes suffered loss of roof and walls, resulting in the area of highest damage being consistent with an EF2 tornado and winds of up to 130 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Line of showers and thunderstorms rapidly developed from northern Ohio to northern lower Michigan early in the afternoon of the 5th in advance of an approaching cold front. Scattered storms within this line became strong to severe as they crossed southeast Michigan leading to areas of wind damage over the northern Metro Detroit area as well as into the eastern Thumb. While this occurred, a spin-up tornado occurred over Livonia resulting in tree damage/downed trees along its path with one large tree falling on a home unfortunately killing a 2 year old boy and critically injuring his mother as they slept.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree landed onto two trucks located on South Duck Lake Road. Two men were injured and take to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough crossing the region amid an unstable summertime environment generated pop-up afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms. Minor wind damage occurred primarily in Antrim and Otsego counties, as well as in Arenac county with a separate cell. Additionally, an unusually moist airmass in place led to some localized rainfall reports in excess of 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of powerlines down in the vicinity of Antrim and Mancelona, accompanied by public reports of dime sized hail and torrential rainfall in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking through the region led to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms through the day. In the afternoon, some of these produced damaging winds in Wexford and Missaukee counties, and also in Ogemaw and Iosco counties. Additionally, locally heavy rainfall occurred with some of these storms, as well as some isolated hail from pea-size up to one inch reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of tree damage and downed powerlines in a swath approximately a mile and a half wide near Dyer Lake east of Falmouth. Trees snapped and uprooted by suspected downburst gusts. Accompanied by torrential rain and up to dime sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms occurred during the early morning hours of July 14th. In the East Jordan area, lightning struck a mobile home, setting it ablaze and resulting in a total loss of the structure. All occupants survived, though all were hospitalized for their injuries, including one occupant who was transported to a larger hospital for treatment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer reported lightning struck a trailer house, setting it ablaze, in the vicinity of East Jordan on the morning of July 14th. This report was corroborated by the Charlevoix Emergency Manager as well as Facebook reports. The structure was two trailer houses joined together; it was a total loss due to the fire. The occupants were all hospitalized...2 were taken to a nearby hospital in Petoskey where they were listed in fair condition; the third had to be transported to a larger hospital in Grand Rapids to receive treatment for his injuries for multiple days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Line of showers and thunderstorms rapidly developed from northern Ohio to northern lower Michigan early in the afternoon of the 5th in advance of an approaching cold front. Scattered storms within this line became strong to severe as they crossed southeast Michigan leading to areas of wind damage over the northern Metro Detroit area as well as into the eastern Thumb. While this occurred, a spin-up tornado occurred over Livonia resulting in tree damage/downed trees along its path with one large tree falling on a home unfortunately killing a 2 year old boy and critically injuring his mother as they slept.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines reported down across St. Clair county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sharp upper-level trough lifted into the region on April 27-28, 2024. Warm, moist airmass advected into the region during the afternoon with gusty synoptic winds finally mixing down with breaks in the clouds late afternoon. Discrete and primarily elevated storms developed ahead of the primary cold front over land during the early evening, with additional development along the cold front moving inland later. A splitting supercell moved onshore into Manistee county; left-moving supercell of this pair resulted in accumulating, damaging large hail in the Manistee-Onekama, MI area. Additional reports of large hail as the front moved eastward through the evening into early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of wind damage, including uprooted trees, during large hail event in Onekama.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Episodic damaging wind gusts occurred from severe thunderstorms moving along the Indiana, Michigan state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports a tree was downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front approached the region during the afternoon/evening of the 29th. This resulted in the development of a line of scattered thunderstorms over northern Michigan during the afternoon into evening hours. One stronger storm at the southern end of the line periodically took on supercell tendencies as it crossed over Traverse City and tracked along M-72 through Kalkaska and Grayling. This resulted in up to dime size hail and wind damage near Williamsburg. Storms continued to produce some gusty winds across northeast Lower Michigan into Lake Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust recorded at mesonet station MC112 MDOT site near Acme.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist atmosphere and slow moving storms resulted in heavy rain over Manistee county, with some localized flooding as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sharp upper-level trough lifted into the region on April 27-28, 2024. Warm, moist airmass advected into the region during the afternoon with gusty synoptic winds finally mixing down with breaks in the clouds late afternoon. Discrete and primarily elevated storms developed ahead of the primary cold front over land during the early evening, with additional development along the cold front moving inland later. A splitting supercell moved onshore into Manistee county; left-moving supercell of this pair resulted in accumulating, damaging large hail in the Manistee-Onekama, MI area. Additional reports of large hail as the front moved eastward through the evening into early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media image of up to inch size hail on a deck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front approached the region during the afternoon/evening of the 29th. This resulted in the development of a line of scattered thunderstorms over northern Michigan during the afternoon into evening hours. One stronger storm at the southern end of the line periodically took on supercell tendencies as it crossed over Traverse City and tracked along M-72 through Kalkaska and Grayling. This resulted in up to dime size hail and wind damage near Williamsburg. Storms continued to produce some gusty winds across northeast Lower Michigan into Lake Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grand Traverse 911 Facebook page reported powerlines down resulting in closure of Williamsburg Road between Crisp Road and Supply Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection fired along and ahead of a trough axis slipping SE across the Upper Peninsula of Michigan Saturday afternoon in the presence of sufficient instability amid a seasonably cold air mass. A couple stronger cells pulsed up and then back down again, producing primarily pea to half-inch size hail and brief heavy rain. One cell produced up to quarter size hail near Brimley around 6pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Facebook of up to quarter size hail east of Brimley, MI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of convection crossed into Michigan during the morning hours of the 25th. A wake low developed behind the northern end of this system, resulting in an area of strong, non-thunderstorm winds crossing the region along and behind the area of remaining rainfall. Widespread gusts of 35-45mph were recorded with this event, particularly along the M-55 corridor toward Lake Huron, along with some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from western Arenac county of backyard chicken equipment shifted a few feet by winds associated with wake low passage, killing 2 chicks in the process.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually moist airmass present over the Upper Midwest in the presence of a surface system and a warm front stretching across central Michigan resulted in the development of circulations embedded within widespread rain...despite lack of lightning due to a deep warm cloud layer. One such circulation tracking along this boundary strengthened as it crossed northern Gladwin County, ultimately resulting in a tornado touchdown near Lake Lancer, ending near the East Branch of the Tittabawassee River. This produced minor home damage near Lake Lancer, as well as tree damage along the rest of its track. Maximum winds estimated to be 90mph, EF-1, with a maximum width of 200 yards. Path length was 10.3 miles and was on the ground for a total of 22 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down in northern Gladwin County, starting near Lake Lancer and ending near the East Branch of the Tittabawassee River. There was some minor home damage around Lake Lancer, with mainly tree damage as it moved east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 14-year-old Toledo, OH boy drowned in Lake Superior on the 24th after being caught in a rip current at a beach near the Lake Superior State Forest Campground in Luce County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14-year-old Toledo, OH boy drowned in Lake Superior on the 24th after being caught in a rip current at a beach near the Lake Superior State Forest Campground.  A 15-year-old was also caught in the rip current but was able to swim to the safety of shallow water.  The Coast Guard, Luce County Sheriff and Michigan DNR conservation officers conducted a search by boat and helicopter until dusk on the evening of the 24th , but waves of 4 to 5 feet at the time of the drowning hindered search and recovery efforts.  The search continued on the next day with additional aid from the Michigan State Police Dive and Drone Team until the victim's body was recovered that afternoon on the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Line of showers and thunderstorms rapidly developed from northern Ohio to northern lower Michigan early in the afternoon of the 5th in advance of an approaching cold front. Scattered storms within this line became strong to severe as they crossed southeast Michigan leading to areas of wind damage over the northern Metro Detroit area as well as into the eastern Thumb. While this occurred, a spin-up tornado occurred over Livonia resulting in tree damage/downed trees along its path with one large tree falling on a home unfortunately killing a 2 year old boy and critically injuring his mother as they slept.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just east of the intersection of Schoolcraft Rd and Eckles Rd and traveled northeast into Mason Rd, just north of I-96. The tornado continued into the intersection of Nola Rd and Lyndon Rd, uprooting large trees, snapping off large tree limbs, and producing some damage to residential housing. The tornado then traveled across Newburgh Rd into Levan Rd near the 5-mile intersection, close to the intersection of Ellen Dr. and 5-mile, where mainly tree damage was found. The tornado continued to travel northeast into Farmington and 6-mile, uprooting trees around this location. The tornado continued through Rotary Park where large trees and tree limbs were sheared off, and then moved into Beverly Park, where the damage was mainly sporadic downed tree limbs. The tornado lifted just southwest of 7 mile and Middlebelt Rd. Maximum wind speeds with this tornado were estimated to be up to 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused an attic fire in Marquette during the evening of the 5th as reported by the Marquette Fire Department.  Nearby tree damage was also reported as the lightning strikes were confirmed by NLDN/GLD lightning data sets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An attic fire reported by the Marquette Fire Department was likely started by lightning as determined by on-the-scene reports and NLDN/GLD lightning data sets.  No injuries were reported from the fire which occurred at a home on 310 Harrison Street in Marquette.  The City of Marquette Fire Department was assisted at the scene by the Marquette Township Fire Department, Chocolay Township Fire Department, the Marquette Police Department, UPHS EMS, SEMCO and the Maquette BLP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally and record dry September into the first half of October led to the ignition of the Summit Lake Wildfire which was discovered on the evening of October 18th in the northwest corner of the McCormick Wilderness between Summit Lake and Evergreen Lake. The McCormick Wilderness is located in Baraga County, 12.5 miles north of Michigamme, MI. The wildfire grew rapidly from 5 acres on the morning of the 19th to 100 acres on the morning of the 20th.  Difficult access for firefighters combined with an abundant mix of hardwood and conifer trees for fuel allowed the wildfire to expand to over 300 acres by the 27th before a combination of snow and rain on the 31st led to its eventual containment by early November.  Dozens of additional wildfires during the very warm and dry October burned over 200 acres across the U.P.  Some of the additional larger fires were the Fox Fire in northern Schoolcraft County which burned 62 acres and one structure, the Section 27 North Fire in Marquette County which burned over 28 acres, and the Round Lake Fire in Alger County that burned nearly 15 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An abnormally and record dry September into the first half of October with precipitation deficits of more than 3 inches led to the ignition of the Summit Lake Wildfire which was discovered on the evening of October 18th in the northwest corner of the McCormick Wilderness between Summit Lake and Evergreen Lake. The McCormick Wilderness is located in Baraga County, 12.5 miles north of Michigamme, MI. The wildfire grew rapidly from 5 acres on the morning of the 19th to 100 acres on the morning of the 20th. Difficult access for firefighters combined with an abundant mix of hardwood and conifer trees for fuel allowed the wildfire to expand over 300 acres by the 27th before a combination of snow and rain on the 31st led to its eventual containment by early November.  Through the efforts of Ottawa National Forest personnel and resources, a Michigan Interagency Type III Incident Management Team and aircraft personnel and resources the cost of fighting the wildfire was estimated at $1.3 million.  Additional wildfires across the U.P. through the month of October resulted in about $750,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the morning of August 6, a supercell developed and tracked east-southeast through southern Michigan, far northeast Indiana and far northwest Ohio. This storm produced an EF2  tornado in northwest St. Joseph county from Lewis Lake to the north side of Three Rivers. This storm did weaken with eastward extent but did maintain some rotation with reports of wall clouds and funnel clouds. Additional thunderstorms then developed east of I-69 Tuesday afternoon, with a few reports of wind damage in northwest Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down at the county line border, just north of Lewis Lake. The tornado did significant tree damage and snapped power poles along the entirety of Berry Ln. The tornado moved southeast towards Day Rd where it reached maximum|width of approximately 700 yards. Here, a well anchored manufactured home was slid approximately five feet off its foundation, along with significant tree damage. Near the|intersection of Bent Rd. and Floating Bridge Rd. the tornado completely destroyed a barn. Farther east on Floating Bridge Rd., the tornado removed a two-story barn roof completely off and moved it about 30 feet away. The tornado reached maximum|intensity of EF-2 along Floating Bridge Rd. Another barn roof received major roof damage, the residence's roof was partially removed, and significant tree damage was also observed. The tornado continued southeast crossing Mount Zion Rd with major|tree damage and center pivots being overturned.  Near Pulver and Null Rd., eight power poles were snapped, an irrigation pivot was twisted, and minor/moderate roof damage was observed to three homes. Farther southeast along Cowling Rd, a well constructed|shed was moved 20-30ft away from its foundation and part of the structure was thrown into the Rocky River. Finally, the tornado caused minor roof damage to a warehouse along N. Main St in Three Rivers. While a portion of the roof was thrown into the trees|across the street, it is believed this is where the tornado terminated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures climbing into the low to mid 90s with dew points in the low to mid 70s Tuesday afternoon lead to heat indices reaching around 100 degrees across most of southeast Michigan. This hot and humid airmass fueled an expanding line of strong to severe thunderstorms which moved through southeast Michigan just before 4 PM and 7 PM. Wind gusts of 40-65 MPH impacted the majority of southeast Michigan, downing tree limbs and trees. Isolated stronger gusts between 66-76 mph were also observed across the Detroit Metro Area. In total, about 380,000 customers lost power in Michigan, with around 210,000 in the DTE service area/southeast Michigan region. Just about every county in southeast Michigan experienced at least minor tree damage. Due to the rapid movement of the line of thunderstorms, rainfall totals were limited to under 1 inch across the majority of the area. Much heavier rainfall occurred with thunderstorms moving through Washtenaw and Wayne counties Tuesday night, leading to total rainfall totals around 3 inches in Ann Arbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet mirco-burst winds downed trees in Plymouth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging thunderstorm wind gusts resulted in downed trees and powerlines in and around the city of Gladwin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in widespread tree damage and power outages throughout Wexford county. This resulted in road closures, including both lanes of M115 near Cadillac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across Southwest Lower Michigan during the evening of Wednesday December 4, 2024. Wind gusts with this front were very strong, resulting in power outages especially near the lakeshore. Precipitation that was a mix of rain and snow near the lakeshore became all snow farther inland as the cold front moved across southern Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 74 mph peak wind gust was recorded at the South Haven regional airport.  Scattered power outages were reported.

Northwest winds gusting as high as 40 mph also caused considerable blowing and drifting of snow, resulting in whiteout conditions at times. Houghton-Keweenaw County Schools were closed on the 5th due to impacts from the lake enhanced snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spilling across Lake Superior continued to generate moderate to occasionally heavy lake effect snow into early December over the northwest to north wind snow belts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Natures Kennel seven miles north of McMillan received 12.5 inches of lake effect snow over the two-day period ending on the morning of the 2nd. The kennel measured a four-day lake effect snowfall total of 40 inches which was confirmed via Facebook comments.  There was also a public report from Newberry of 19 inches of lake effect snow over the 72-hour period ending at noon on the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spilling across Lake Superior continued to generate moderate to occasionally heavy lake effect snow into early December over the northwest to north wind snow belts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers in Munising and near Forest Lakes measured just over 10 inches of lake effect snow while Wetmore received 8 inches in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front tracked across southeast Michigan during the evening hours of December 4th, leading to numerous snow squalls and peak wind gusts of 40 mph to localized 50+ mph. Snowfall accumulations within these snow showers varied widely across the CWA, but storm totals generally fell within the 1 to 4 inch range. The exception to this was in the Thumb region where a deformation axis/pivot point existed downwind of an ice-free Saginaw Bay. Snowfall reports in Huron County ranged between 8 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in Huron couny, with the bulk of the snow falling in under 8 hours. Official snow reports of 9 inches were reported in Caseville and 8.2 inches in Bad Axe. Strong winds gusting above 35 mph lead to a significant amount of blowing and drifting snow as well, with severly reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and deep cyclonic flow bringing unseasonably cold weather overtop unusually warm lake surface temperatures continued to result in record-breaking lake effect snow through the remainder of the Thanksgiving weekend into December 1-2. This brought periods of whiteout conditions and hazardous travel conditions as snow bands shifted back and forth over the region. Snow under these most intense bands quickly accumulated to the tune of 1-3 inches per hour, even on main thoroughfares including I-75...resulting in numerous accidents. Widespread 4-day snowfall totals of 2-4 feet in NW/WNW flow snowband areas of NW Lower and eastern Upper Michigan. Highest reported storm total snowfall from trained snowfall observers was 46.7 inches 6 miles SSW of Gaylord, MI, ending 1300EST December 2. This event broke all-time snowfall records for 1-day, 2-day, and 3-day snowfall totals for the Gaylord area. Reliable snowfall records for the Gaylord, MI area go back to 1940. For the Sault Ste. Marie, MI area, this was the 3rd highest snowfall event on record, with a storm total of 29.7 inches from 0000EST Thanksgiving morning through December 2, trailing behind the December 1995 4-day lake effect event, and Christmas blizzard 2022 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four hour total of 13.8 inches measured at East Jordan Wastewater Treatment Plant ending on the morning of the 30th, with storm totals through December 2nd of 21.9 inches. As an example of lake effect snowfall gradients, the cooperative observer 2 miles NW of East Jordan received only 10.2 inches through the entire 72+hour event ending the morning of December 2nd. Twenty-four hour snowfall of 8 inches ending on the morning of the 30th, from Boyne Falls cooperative observer. Storm total for this location through December 2 of 15.0 inches. Event totals as high as 16 to 17 inches in the Boyne Falls area by the morning of the 30th per social media reports...with higher totals likely elsewhere in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of lake effect snow, cold temperatures and gusty winds created difficult travel late December 4th into December 5th. Snowfall totals up to 10 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic front plunged through during the evening hours on December 4th with bouts of moderate snow and wind gusts in excess of 45 mph. Near zero visibility and a flash freeze were reported. A transition to pure lake effect snow then took place on December 5th. The worst of the conditions developed during the afternoon and evening hours as a dominant single snow band settled in for a time with heavy snowfall rates, reduced visibilities and snow covered roads. Snowfall totals varied over the area, generally ranging between 2 and 6 inches, with 4.8��� of snow reported 2 miles northeast of Three Rivers. There were also reports of vehicular accidents in the area.

Reports of whiteout conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moving into the region on December 4th brought more unseasonably cold air over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, allowing lake effect influences to ramp up again. Some freezing drizzle was noted ahead of the clipper amid warm advection into the afternoon on the 4th, before a more potent area of snow crossed the area during the late afternoon, transitioning to intense NW flow lake effect snow bands by late evening as winds increased in the wake of the front, gusting as high as 53mph at Frankfort and Leland harbors. Snowfall totals under the heaviest bands by the morning of the 5th were largely 7-12 inches, with blowing and drifting snow further impacting travel conditions across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer 2 miles ESE of Elmira in NW Otsego county reported snowfall of 8.2 inches ending 0700EST on the 5th. Additional reports from Otsego county, including a CoCoRaHS observer 4 miles S of Gaylord and 24-hr snowfall totals ending on the morning of the 5th at the NWS Gaylord, MI office 9 miles SSW of Gaylord, indicate broad snowfall totals of 7-8 inches across the county. Some freezing rain occurred during the middle of the day on the 4th, resulting in a glaze of ice on most surfaces.

Areas east of this toward Michaywe and west toward Lake Manuka reported in excess of 2-3 feet of snow through the event. By 1300EST on the 1st, 39.6 inches of snow had fallen at this location, with 8 inches alone in the previous 24 hours.

This event broke all-time snowfall records for 1-day, 2-day, and 3-day snowfall totals for the Gaylord area. Reliable snowfall records for the Gaylord, MI area go back to 1940. For the Sault Ste. Marie, MI area, this was the 3rd highest snowfall event on record, with a storm total of 29.7 inches from 0000EST Thanksgiving morning through December 2, trailing behind the December 1995 4-day lake effect event, and Christmas blizzard 2022 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reports from the Epsilon area in SE Emmet county of up to 25 inches, storm totals, by the morning of December 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and deep cyclonic flow bringing unseasonably cold weather overtop unusually warm lake surface temperatures continued to result in record-breaking lake effect snow through the remainder of the Thanksgiving weekend into December 1-2. This brought periods of whiteout conditions and hazardous travel conditions as snow bands shifted back and forth over the region. Snow under these most intense bands quickly accumulated to the tune of 1-3 inches per hour, even on main thoroughfares including I-75...resulting in numerous accidents. Widespread 4-day snowfall totals of 2-4 feet in NW/WNW flow snowband areas of NW Lower and eastern Upper Michigan. Highest reported storm total snowfall from trained snowfall observers was 46.7 inches 6 miles SSW of Gaylord, MI, ending 1300EST December 2. This event broke all-time snowfall records for 1-day, 2-day, and 3-day snowfall totals for the Gaylord area. Reliable snowfall records for the Gaylord, MI area go back to 1940. For the Sault Ste. Marie, MI area, this was the 3rd highest snowfall event on record, with a storm total of 29.7 inches from 0000EST Thanksgiving morning through December 2, trailing behind the December 1995 4-day lake effect event, and Christmas blizzard 2022 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to snowfall from earlier in the weekend, cooperative observer in Grayling, MI reported snowfall total of 10.0 inches ending the morning of December 2. Social media reports during the event indicated the bulk of this may have fallen between noon and 3pm before band pivoted to the west in the late afternoon/evening. This resulted in sloppy and slick roads lingering into the evening, even on freeways, with multiple accidents on I-75 between Roscommon and Grayling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moving into the region on December 4th brought more unseasonably cold air over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, allowing lake effect influences to ramp up again. Some freezing drizzle was noted ahead of the clipper amid warm advection into the afternoon on the 4th, before a more potent area of snow crossed the area during the late afternoon, transitioning to intense NW flow lake effect snow bands by late evening as winds increased in the wake of the front, gusting as high as 53mph at Frankfort and Leland harbors. Snowfall totals under the heaviest bands by the morning of the 5th were largely 7-12 inches, with blowing and drifting snow further impacting travel conditions across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer 1 mile N of Wellston reported 9.5 inches of snow by mid morning on the 5th, accompanied by strong gusty winds and blowing snow overnight. 6 inches of this fell prior to 0100EST on the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level troughing digging into the region brought another round of Arctic air overtop anomalously warm lake temperatures for mid-December, resulting in another round of impressive lake enhanced snowfall totals across the region. Lighter background flow initially resulted in development of disorganized lake effect/enhanced snow bands, including a couple mesolows...one which crossed northern Lower Michigan during the day on the 11th, and another that developed over the UP along lake aggregate troughing on the 11th, which was ejected SE into the morning of the 12th as a more focused trough axis swung into the region. This was followed by more stereotypical NW flow lake effect for the day on the 12th. This resulted in periods of hazardous travel, and snowfall totals close to 24 inches in 24-48 hours. By 1900EST on the 12th, total snowfall for the 2024-25 winter season at the NWS Gaylord, MI office was 83 inches, over half of the normal snowfall for an entire winter season for the Gaylord area. 81.2 inches of this fell since 0000EST on November 28th (Thanksgiving Day).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals for Charlevoix county of 12-20 inches, corroborated by cooperative observer reports at East Jordan Wastewater Treatment Plant and Boyne Falls by December 12th of 12.2 and 14.0 inches, respectively. Additional public reports of 15-20 inches in the vicinity of Walloon Lake, which was largely a pivot point for shifting snow bands through the night of the 11th-12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rounds of lake effect snow showers produced snow accumulations in the 6 to 12 inch range across southwest Lower Michigan.  The first round developed from late morning to early evening on December 2nd, with the second round during the early morning hours of December 3rd.  Travel was adversely affected with reports of slide-offs and accidents across southwest Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate to heavy lake effect snow showers moved in during the early afternoon hours on December 2nd. Snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour were reported at times.  Numerous slide-offs were reported.  Another round of lake effect snow showers with additional snow accumulations occurred during the early to mid morning hours of December 3rd before tapering by late morning.  Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 9 inches in association with the heavier snow bands from both rounds, mainly across the far northwest portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of lake effect snow, cold temperatures and gusty winds created difficult travel late December 4th into December 5th. Snowfall totals up to 10 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic front plunged through during the evening hours on December 4th with bouts of heavy snow and wind gusts in excess of 45 mph. Near zero visibility and a flash freeze were reported. A transition to pure lake effect snow then took place on December 5th. The worst of the conditions developed during the afternoon and evening hours as a dominant single snow band settled in for a time with heavy snowfall rates, reduced visibilities and snow covered roads. Snowfall totals varied over the area, generally ranging between 4 and 10 inches, heaviest near Paw Paw Lake. There were also reports of vehicular accidents in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cyclonic flow settled into the Upper Great Lakes starting on Thanksgiving night as the first in a series of disturbances moved into the region. Upper level troughing then settled in for the weekend, spanning the last couple days of November and the first couple days of December, bringing an unseasonably cold air mass over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, resulting in an extended period of significant lake effect snow. Despite wind shifts that shifted the position of the most intense snow bands through the weekend, 1-3inch per hour snowfall rates within these bands resulted in 12hour totals of 12inches or more. The NWS Gaylord, MI office, located 9miles SSW of Gaylord, set an all-time calendar-day snowfall record of 24.8inches for the calendar date of 11-29-2024, with more to come through the next 3 days into December 2. Some areas of Otsego County received as much as 4feet of snow through the entire event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter in Indian River reported 12 hour total of 11.3 inches ending 2100EST on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cyclonic flow settled into the Upper Great Lakes starting on Thanksgiving night as the first in a series of disturbances moved into the region. Upper level troughing then settled in for the weekend, spanning the last couple days of November and the first couple days of December, bringing an unseasonably cold air mass over the unseasonably warm Great Lakes, resulting in an extended period of significant lake effect snow. Despite wind shifts that shifted the position of the most intense snow bands through the weekend, 1-3inch per hour snowfall rates within these bands resulted in 12hour totals of 12inches or more. The NWS Gaylord, MI office, located 9miles SSW of Gaylord, set an all-time calendar-day snowfall record of 24.8inches for the calendar date of 11-29-2024, with more to come through the next 3 days into December 2. Some areas of Otsego County received as much as 4feet of snow through the entire event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow began on the 28th, with accumulations around a foot by morning on the 29th...and enough to bring snow totals up to 20 inches by 4pm. By the afternoon of December 1st, 60-hour snowfall totals from Sugar Island reached as high as 42 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer at Tahquamenon Falls State Park, MI, about 8miles west of Paradise, MI, recorded a 72-hour snowfall total of 22.5 inches ending December 1st with primarily WNW flow lake effect snow bands. Highest snowfall totals during this period occurred on the 28th and 30th, with both days' 24-hour totals around 9 inches. Snow depth by December 1st was 26 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee 4miles SSE of Elmira, MI reported a storm total of 46 inches ending 0900EST December 2. By 1300EST on the 1st, 39.6inches of snow had fallen at this location, with 8inches alone in the previous 24hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low pressure system moved into the Upper Great Lakes late in the day on November 25, 2024, bringing accumulating wet snow to the eastern U.P. of Michigan. Accumulations were minimized south of the Mackinac Bridge due to warmer temperatures and precipitation falling as rain or a mix at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snowfall in the Trout Lake area overnight, reported via social media.

As an example of lake effect snowfall gradients, the cooperative observer 2miles NW of East Jordan received only 10.2 inches through the entire 72+hour event ending the morning of December 2nd.
